paper_summary.summary: A framework leverages "negative data" – incorrect rationales generated by large language models – to enhance the complex arithmetic reasoning capabilities of smaller language models. This approach achieved an approximate 75.75% increase in accuracy on arithmetic benchmarks and improved out-of-distribution performance.

title: Turning Dust into Gold: Distilling Complex Reasoning Capabilities from LLMs by Leveraging Negative Data

abstract: Long chain-of-thought (CoT) supervision has become a common strategy to enhance reasoning in language models. While effective for large models, we identify a phenomenon we call Long CoT Degradation, in which small language models (SLMs; <=3B parameters) trained on limited long CoT data experience significant performance deterioration. Through extensive experiments on the Qwen2.5, LLaMA3 and Gemma3 families, we demonstrate that this degradation is widespread across SLMs. In some settings, models trained on only 8k long CoT examples lose up to 75% of their original performance before fine-tuning. Strikingly, we further observe that for some particularly small models, even training on 220k long CoT examples fails to recover or surpass their original performance prior to fine-tuning. Our analysis attributes this effect to error accumulation: while longer responses increase the capacity for multi-step reasoning, they also amplify the risk of compounding mistakes. Furthermore, we find that Long CoT Degradation may negatively impacts downstream reinforcement learning (RL), although this can be alleviated by sufficiently scaled supervised fine-tuning (SFT). Our findings challenge common assumptions about the benefits of long CoT training for SLMs and offer practical guidance for building more effective small-scale reasoning models.

organization_info.name: Singapore University of Technology and Design

title: Through the Valley: Path to Effective Long CoT Training for Small Language Models

abstract: Distilling the capabilities from a large reasoning model (LRM) to a smaller student model often involves training on substantial amounts of reasoning data. However, knowledge distillation (KD) over lengthy sequences with prompt (P), chain-of-thought (CoT), and answer (A) sections makes the process computationally expensive. In this work, we investigate how the allocation of supervision across different sections (P, CoT, A) affects student performance. Our analysis shows that selective KD over only the CoT tokens can be effective when the prompt and answer information is encompassed by it. Building on this insight, we establish a truncation protocol to quantify computation-quality tradeoffs as a function of sequence length. We observe that beyond a specific length, longer training sequences provide marginal returns for downstream performance but require substantially higher memory and FLOPs. To this end, training on only the first $50\%$ of tokens of every training sequence can retain, on average, $\approx91\%$ of full-sequence performance on math benchmarks while reducing training time, memory usage, and FLOPs by about $50\%$ each. Codes are available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) tokens are the most critical supervisory signal for transferring reasoning capabilities, with additional supervision from Prompt or Answer segments offering negligible benefits.

Student model performance saturates early, with diminishing returns for training on full-length reasoning sequences, indicating that much of the later CoT content is redundant or less informative for distillation.
The most valuable reasoning signals and critical derivation steps are concentrated in the initial segments of the CoT, supporting the concept of 'overthinking' in longer traces.
originalProblem: The high computational cost and resource demands of distilling reasoning capabilities from large teacher LLMs to smaller student models, especially with lengthy Chain-of-Thought (CoT) traces.
Lack of clarity on which specific sequence segments (Prompt, CoT, Answer) are most crucial for transferring reasoning knowledge during distillation.
The implicit assumption that supervising entire long CoT sequences is necessary, potentially leading to redundancy and inefficiency.
results: Supervising only the Chain-of-Thought (CoT) segment proved to be the most effective, with Prompt and Answer segments contributing minimally when CoT was already included.

Truncating training sequences to their initial 50% of tokens retained approximately 91% of the full-sequence performance on math benchmarks, while reducing training time, GPU memory, and FLOPs by around 50%.
Training with the first 50% of tokens consistently and significantly outperformed training with the final 50%, underscoring the denser learning signal in early reasoning steps.
solution: Implemented a supervised knowledge distillation framework using Qwen3 models, systematically applying loss masks across different sequence segments (Prompt, CoT, Answer).
Investigated the impact of sequence length by truncating training examples to various "Lead-Span Proportions" (LSP), focusing on early token segments.
Conducted a "Budget Location Ablation" study, comparing distillation performance when supervising the first 50% versus the last 50% of tokens, complemented by linguistic analysis.
paper_summary.summary: Research identifies that truncating reasoning sequences to their initial 50% for distillation reduces training costs by half while preserving approximately 91% of the full-sequence performance. The findings highlight the Chain-of-Thought segment as the main source of supervisory signal, with early tokens carrying the most critical reasoning information.

title: Distilling the Essence: Efficient Reasoning Distillation via Sequence Truncation

abstract: Recent studies have shown that reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) enhances overall accuracy (pass@1) but often fails to improve capability (pass@k) of LLMs in reasoning tasks, while distillation can improve both. In this paper, we investigate the mechanisms behind these phenomena. First, we demonstrate that RLVR struggles to improve capability as it focuses on improving the accuracy of the easier questions to the detriment of the accuracy of the most difficult questions. Second, we show that RLVR does not merely increase the success probability for the easier questions, but in our small model settings, produces quality responses that were absent in its original output distribution. In addition, we show these responses are neither noticeably longer nor feature more reflection-related keywords, underscoring the need for more reliable indicators of response quality. Third, from the experiment distilling teacher responses to in-distribution problems, we find that capability does not always improve with distillation. We conjecture that capability improves only when new knowledge is introduced, whereas distilling reasoning patterns only improves accuracy but not capability, sacrificing performance on the most difficult questions, similar to RLVR. Together, these findings offer a clearer understanding of how RLVR and distillation shape reasoning behavior in LLMs

keyInsights: RLVR's accuracy gains primarily stem from improved performance on easier questions, often at the expense of ignoring or worsening outcomes for truly difficult problems.
Despite not expanding overall capability, RLVR enables LLMs to produce qualitatively higher-quality correct responses that were not part of the base model's initial output distribution.
Capability expansion through distillation largely depends on the transfer of new domain knowledge from the teacher model, whereas distilling only reasoning patterns primarily boosts accuracy.
For smaller LLMs, surface-level indicators like response length or reflection-related keywords do not reliably correlate with improved reasoning quality, challenging common evaluation assumptions.
originalProblem: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) improves LLM accuracy (pass@1) but often fails to expand underlying problem-solving capability (pass@k) in general reasoning tasks.
It remained unclear whether RLVR genuinely broadens an LLM's fundamental ability to solve new problems or simply improves the model's consistency on already solvable ones.

The conditions under which distillation truly enhances an LLM's reasoning capability, specifically distinguishing the roles of transferring reasoning patterns versus introducing new domain knowledge, were not well understood.
results: RLVR consistently improved pass@1 but not pass@k, with gains concentrated on questions where the base model already had a moderate success rate, exhibiting negligible improvement on the most difficult problems.

Self-distillation experiments demonstrated that distilling the base model with RL model-generated correct responses led to significant accuracy gains, confirming RLVR's production of higher-quality outputs.

The DeepSeek distilled model, likely containing new domain knowledge, showed clear capability expansion across all difficulty bins. In contrast, the custom 'reasoning-only' distilled model improved accuracy but not capability, mimicking RLVR's behavior.
solution: The study employed an empirical methodology using Qwen2.5 models and mathematical reasoning datasets, defining and meticulously distinguishing between accuracy (pass@1) and capability (pass@k).

RLVR's impact was analyzed by categorizing questions based on base model difficulty, examining success rate transitions, measuring output distribution entropy, and conducting self-distillation experiments.

Distillation's effects were investigated by comparing a general distilled model (incorporating new knowledge) with a custom 'reasoning-only' distilled model (isolating reasoning pattern transfer) on pass@k and difficulty-stratified performance.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from NYU Abu Dhabi empirically demonstrate that Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) primarily enhances LLM reasoning accuracy by optimizing performance on easier questions, without expanding underlying capability. In contrast, distillation improves capability when it incorporates new domain knowledge from a teacher model, rather than merely transferring reasoning patterns.

title: Reinforcement Learning vs. Distillation: Understanding Accuracy and Capability in LLM Reasoning

abstract: Improving the reasoning abilities of large language models (LLMs) has largely relied on iterative self-training with model-generated data. While effective at boosting accuracy, existing approaches primarily reinforce successful reasoning paths, incurring a substantial calibration cost: models become overconfident and lose the ability to represent uncertainty. This failure has been characterized as a form of model collapse in alignment, where predictive distributions degenerate toward low-variance point estimates. We address this issue by reframing reasoning training as an epistemic learning problem, in which models must learn not only how to reason, but also when their reasoning should be trusted. We propose epistemically-calibrated reasoning (EpiCaR) as a training objective that jointly optimizes reasoning performance and calibration, and instantiate it within an iterative supervised fine-tuning framework using explicit self-evaluation signals. Experiments on Llama-3 and Qwen-3 families demonstrate that our approach achieves Pareto-superiority over standard baselines in both accuracy and calibration, particularly in models with sufficient reasoning capacity (e.g., 3B+). This framework generalizes effectively to OOD mathematical reasoning (GSM8K) and code generation (MBPP). Ultimately, our approach enables a 3X reduction in inference compute, matching the K=30 performance of STaR with only K=10 samples in capable models.

keyInsights: Reframing reasoning training as an "epistemic learning problem" allows models to learn not only how to reason but also when their reasoning should be trusted.
Integrating negative self-evaluation signals during training is crucial for mitigating model collapse and the "calibration cost" often associated with positive-only self-training methods.
Uncertainty calibration, when intrinsically learned, serves as a fundamental prerequisite for developing compute-optimal reasoning systems, rather than just an external adjustment.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) often exhibit overconfidence and miscalibration, providing incorrect answers with high certainty, which undermines trustworthiness.
Existing iterative self-training methods (e.g., STaR) improve accuracy but incur a "calibration cost," leading to overconfident model collapse by only reinforcing successful paths.
Inference-time scaling techniques like Self-Consistency enhance reliability but come with substantial computational overhead, limiting practical deployment.
results: EPICAR achieves Pareto-superiority over baselines like STaR, simultaneously improving both reasoning accuracy and calibration (e.g., lower ECE, higher AUROC) on Llama-3 and Qwen-3 families (3B+ models).
It demonstrates robust reliability generalization to out-of-distribution mathematical reasoning (GSM8K) and cross-domain code generation (MBPP), maintaining strong discriminative power.
EPICAR-trained models enable a 3x reduction in inference compute, matching the performance of high-sample-count self-consistency methods with significantly fewer samples (e.g., K=10 vs. K=30).
solution: EPICAR introduces an iterative dual-loop supervised fine-tuning (SFT) framework that jointly optimizes reasoning and uncertainty calibration.
It utilizes explicitly self-evaluated incorrect reasoning paths as negative feedback, incorporated into a `D_eval` dataset, alongside correct paths (`D_reason`), for combined training.

Adaptive Injection Decoding (AID) is employed during generation to ensure format compliance, preventing mislabeling of logically correct but poorly formatted outputs and preserving the integrity of self-evaluation signals.
paper_summary.summary: The EPICAR framework, developed by researchers at Seoul National University, introduces an epistemic learning objective for large language models to jointly optimize reasoning performance and uncertainty calibration. It achieves Pareto-superiority in both metrics and enables a 3x reduction in inference compute by effectively integrating negative self-evaluation signals during supervised fine-tuning.

title: EpiCaR: Knowing What You Don't Know Matters for Better Reasoning in LLMs

abstract: Transferring reasoning capabilities from larger language models to smaller ones through supervised fine-tuning often fails counterintuitively, with performance degrading despite access to high-quality teacher demonstrations. We identify that this failure stems from distributional misalignment: reasoning traces from larger models contain tokens that are low probability under the student's distribution, exceeding the internal representation capacity of smaller architectures and creating learning barriers rather than helpful guidance. We propose Reverse Speculative Decoding (RSD), a mechanism for generating student-friendly reasoning traces in which the teacher model proposes candidate tokens but the student model determines acceptance based on its own probability distributions, filtering low probability tokens. When applied to Qwen3-0.6B, direct distillation of s1K-1.1 reasoning trace data degrades average performance across major reasoning benchmarks by 20.5\%, while the same model trained on RSD-generated reasoning traces achieves meaningful improvements of 4.9\%. Our analysis reveals that low probability tokens constitute the critical bottleneck in reasoning ability transfer. However, cross-model experiments demonstrate that RSD traces are model-specific rather than universally applicable, indicating that distributional alignment must be tailored for each student architecture's unique internal representation.

keyInsights: Performance degradation in small models during reasoning SFT is primarily caused by distributional misalignment, specifically the presence of low-probability tokens in teacher-generated traces.
Effective reasoning transfer to small models is model-specific; traces tailored for one student model do not universally benefit or may even degrade the performance of other models.

RSD successfully filters high-surprisal tokens, which frequently correspond to complex logical connectors or transitional keywords, without simplifying the underlying logical complexity of the reasoning steps.
originalProblem: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with high-quality teacher traces often counterintuitively degrades reasoning performance in substantially smaller language models.

Reasoning traces generated by large models contain token sequences that fall into very low probability regions under a smaller student model's distribution, acting as 'high-surprisal' learning barriers.
Existing distillation methods fail to account for the inherent representational capacity and linguistic patterns of small models, leading to distributional misalignment during knowledge transfer.
results: SFT on standard teacher traces degraded `Qwen3-0.6B` performance by 20.5%, whereas RSD improved it by an average of 4.9% (to 27.21% accuracy) using a 1% probability threshold.
RSD effectively reduced the ratio of sub-1% probability tokens in traces from 6.70% (in s1K-1.1) to 0.09% (in RSD with 1% p_th), directly correlating with improved performance.

RSD achieved meaningful improvements with a small dataset (1,000 problems, 180 full traces), demonstrating efficiency in identifying and removing specific learning barriers over relying on massive data.
solution: Proposed Reverse Speculative Decoding (RSD), an algorithm where a teacher model proposes tokens, but a student model only accepts them if their probability under its own distribution exceeds a predefined threshold (e.g., 1%).
Employs rejection sampling to ensure that RSD-generated traces are not only distributionally aligned with the student but also logically correct.

Utilizes a hybrid training approach: applying standard SFT on complete RSD-generated traces and Unsupervised Prefix Fine-Tuning (UPFT) on initial segments of problems where no complete RSD solution was found.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Yonsei University introduced Reverse Speculative Decoding (RSD), a method to generate student-friendly reasoning traces that are distributionally aligned for small language models. This approach overcomes performance degradation observed with direct distillation, achieving an average accuracy increase of 4.9% for a Qwen3-0.6B model on complex reasoning benchmarks.

title: In Their Own Words: Reasoning Traces Tailored for Small Models Make Them Better Reasoners

abstract: In reasoning tasks, even a minor error can cascade into inaccurate results, leading to suboptimal performance of large language models in such domains. Earlier fine-tuning approaches sought to mitigate this by leveraging more precise supervisory signals from human labeling, larger models, or self-sampling, although at a high cost. Conversely, we develop a method that avoids external resources, relying instead on introducing perturbations to the input. Our training approach randomly masks certain tokens within the chain of thought, a technique we found to be particularly effective for reasoning tasks. When applied to fine-tuning with GSM8K on Llama-2-7B, this method achieved a 5\% improvement in GSM8K accuracy and a 10\% improvement in GSM-IC accuracy over standard supervised fine-tuning with a few codes modified. Furthermore, it is complementary to existing methods. When integrated with related explicit data augmentation methods, it leads to improvements across five datasets of various augmentation methods, as well as two different base models. We further investigate the mechanisms behind this improvement through case studies and quantitative analysis, suggesting that our approach may provide superior support for the model in capturing long-distance dependencies, especially those related to questions. This enhancement could deepen understanding of the premises in questions and prior steps. Our code is available at Github.
title: Masked Thought: Simply Masking Partial Reasoning Steps Can Improve Mathematical Reasoning Learning of Language Models

abstract:
Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) often rely on generating intermediate reasoning steps to enhance accuracy. However, little work has examined how reasoning utility contributes to the final answer's correctness. Due to the stochastic nature of autoregressive generation, generating more context does not guarantee increased confidence in the answer. If we could predict, during generation, whether a reasoning step will be useful, we could stop early or prune ineffective steps, avoiding distractions in the final decision.
We present an oracle study on MATH dataset, using Qwen2.5-32B and GPT-4o to generate reasoning chains, and then employing a separate model (Qwen3-8B) to quantify the utility of these chains for final accuracy. Specifically, we measure the model's uncertainty on the answer span Y at each reasoning step using conditional entropy (expected negative log-likelihood over the vocabulary) with context expanding step by step. Our results show a clear pattern: conditional entropy that decreases over steps is strongly associated with correct answers, whereas flat or increasing entropy often results in wrong answers. We also corroborate that incorrect reasoning paths tend to be longer than correct ones, suggesting that longer reasoning does not necessarily yield better outcomes. These findings serve as a foundation to inspire future work on designing efficient reasoning pipelines that detect and avoid unproductive reasoning early.

keyInsights: A consistent decrease in conditional entropy on the answer span reliably indicates effective reasoning and correlates strongly with correct final answers.

Longer LLM reasoning chains, especially those leading to incorrect answers, are often inefficient and do not translate to better outcomes, challenging the 'more steps equal better reasoning' assumption.
Human reasoning strategies differ from LLMs, characterized by a rapid, early reduction in uncertainty compared to the LLMs' more gradual approach.
Commonly used metrics like semantic similarity and cross-entropy are unreliable for gauging reasoning utility or predicting answer correctness in LLMs.
originalProblem: LLMs often generate computationally expensive and lengthy reasoning chains without guaranteeing proportional accuracy gains, leading to inefficiency.
There is a lack of reliable, internal, and training-free mechanisms to assess the utility of individual reasoning steps during LLM generation.
LLMs can get 'distracted' by unproductive reasoning, potentially leading to errors and longer processing times.
results: Correct LLM reasoning paths exhibit a consistent and significant decrease in conditional entropy, while incorrect paths show flat or even rising entropy.
Incorrect LLM reasoning paths are notably longer than correct ones across all tested math categories, and LLM solutions are generally longer than human solutions.
Human reasoning trajectories demonstrate a sharp initial drop in uncertainty, quickly narrowing the answer space, whereas correct LLM trajectories show a more uniform, gradual decrease.
Semantic similarity and cross-entropy proved ineffective as utility indicators, with LLMs sometimes expressing high confidence (low cross-entropy) in incorrect answers.
solution: The research proposes using conditional entropy reduction on the answer span as an information-theoretic metric to quantify reasoning utility.

An 'oracle study' setup leverages an inspector LLM (Qwen3-8B) to estimate token and sequence-level conditional entropy for various reasoning trajectories generated by other LLMs (GPT-4o, Qwen2.5-32B) on the MATH dataset.

Entropy trajectories are generated and aligned using interpolation to analyze how uncertainty evolves across incremental reasoning steps, comparing correct vs. incorrect LLM answers and human reasoning.
paper_summary.summary: This study introduces conditional entropy reduction as an internal, training-free metric to quantify the utility of reasoning steps in Large Language Models (LLMs). It demonstrates a strong correlation between the decrease in this entropy and the correctness of the final answer, revealing that incorrect reasoning paths typically show no such reduction and are often longer than correct ones.

title: Measuring Reasoning Utility in LLMs via Conditional Entropy Reduction

abstract: Uncertainty quantification for LLMs is a key research direction towards addressing hallucination and other issues that limit their reliable deployment. In this work, we show that reasoning trace length is a simple and useful confidence estimator in large reasoning models. Through comprehensive experiments across multiple models, datasets, and prompts, we show that trace length performs in comparable but complementary ways to other zero-shot confidence estimators such as verbalized confidence. Our work reveals that reasoning post-training fundamentally alters the relationship between trace length and accuracy, going beyond prior work that had shown that post-training causes traces to grow longer in general (e.g., "overthinking"). We investigate the mechanisms behind trace length's performance as a confidence signal, observing that the effect remains even after adjusting for confounders such as problem difficulty and GRPO-induced length bias. We identify high-entropy or "forking" tokens as playing a key role in the mechanism. Our findings demonstrate that reasoning post-training enhances uncertainty quantification beyond verbal expressions, and establish trace length as a practical confidence measure for large reasoning models.

keyInsights: Reasoning post-training is essential for trace length to emerge as a reliable zero-shot confidence signal, a property not observed in base models.
High-entropy 'forking tokens' within a reasoning trace are strongly correlated with its length and function as an independent, effective uncertainty signal.
Trace length and verbalized confidence capture distinct yet complementary aspects of model uncertainty, making their combination beneficial for improved UQ.
originalProblem: Quantifying uncertainty in Large Language Models (LLMs) is crucial for enhancing their reliability and trustworthiness.
Mitigating issues such as hallucination and factual inaccuracy in LLM outputs requires effective confidence estimation.
A need exists for efficient, zero-shot uncertainty quantification methods that do not require additional training or complex prompt modifications.
results: Reasoning trace length provided a zero-shot uncertainty signal that performed comparably to verbalized confidence, typically within 2-3 AUROC points across models and datasets.
A simple combination of trace length and verbalized confidence (VC+TL) consistently outperformed either measure alone, serving as the best method in 110 out of 120 cases for 32B models.
The utility of trace length as a UQ signal persists even when models are not explicitly prompted for confidence and is not solely attributable to problem difficulty or GRPO-induced length bias.
solution: Investigated the length of a reasoning trace as a simple, emergent, zero-shot signal for an LLM's confidence in its answer, particularly after reasoning post-training.
Systematically compared trace length's performance against verbalized confidence, a prominent zero-shot UQ method, across various models and datasets.
Explored the underlying mechanisms contributing to trace length's efficacy as an uncertainty signal, including the role of 'forking tokens,' problem difficulty, and GRPO-induced length bias.
paper_summary.summary: Reasoning trace length serves as an emergent, zero-shot confidence indicator in large reasoning models after post-training, performing comparably to explicit verbalized confidence. This intrinsic signal, linked to high-entropy "forking tokens," can be combined with verbalized confidence to enhance uncertainty quantification.

title: Trace Length is a Simple Uncertainty Signal in Reasoning Models

abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR)-based post-training of Large Language Models (LLMs) has been shown to improve accuracy on reasoning tasks and continues to attract significant attention. Existing RLVR methods, however, typically treat all tokens uniformly without accounting for token-level advantages. These methods primarily evaluate performance based on final answer correctness or Pass@K accuracy, and yet make claims about RL post-training leading to improved reasoning traces. This motivates our investigation into the effect of RL post-training on intermediate tokens which are not directly incentivized. To study this, we design an experimental setup using the GRPO algorithm with Qwen-2.5-0.5B model on the GSM8K dataset. We introduce trace coherence, a First-Order Logic (FOL)-based measure to capture the consistency of reasoning steps by identifying errors in the traces. We distinguish between trace validity and trace coherence, noting that the former implies logical soundness while the latter measures local coherence via lack of errors. Our results show that RL post-training overall improves trace coherence with the most significant gains on problems where the base model fails but the RL model succeeds. Surprisingly, RL enhances local coherence without necessarily producing valid or correct solutions. This highlights a crucial distinction: improved local coherence in reasoning steps does not guarantee final answer correctness. We argue that claims of improved reasoning via RL must be examined with care, as these may be based on improved trace coherence, which may not translate into fully valid mathematical proofs.

keyInsights: RLVR primarily enhances "local coherence" by reducing identifiable errors within individual reasoning steps, especially when it leads to a correct final answer.

A crucial distinction exists between improved local coherence and "global validity"; local consistency does not automatically guarantee the overall logical soundness or correctness of the entire reasoning process.

Current RLVR mechanisms, which typically reward based on final answer correctness, may be optimizing for superficial local consistency rather than fostering a deeper, more robust global understanding or proof structure.
originalProblem: Despite the widespread use of RLVR for LLM post-training and claims of "improved reasoning," its specific impact on the *intermediate reasoning traces* generated by LLMs remained largely unexamined.

Existing RLVR methods primarily incentivize *final answer correctness* through uniform reward distribution, providing no direct mechanism to assess or reward the quality and logical soundness of individual reasoning steps.

A systematic method was needed to evaluate the step-by-step reasoning quality of RLVR-trained LLMs, especially for complex problems like those in GSM8K where formal verification of every intermediate step is intractable.
results: RLVR post-training generally improved trace coherence across various Pass@K evaluations (Pass@1, Pass@4, Pass@16) compared to the base model.

In cases where the RL model successfully corrected a base model error (Pattern 01), it achieved approximately 85% trace coherence, indicating significant gains in reasoning quality when RLVR enables problem-solving.

Even for problems where both models produced correct final answers (Pattern 11), the RL model showed consistent improvement, reaching up to 96% trace coherence, suggesting a refinement of already correct solutions.
solution: A novel First-Order Logic (FOL)-based error taxonomy was developed to define and measure "trace coherence"—the absence of errors in reasoning steps—serving as a proxy for reasoning quality in mathematical domains.
The Qwen-2.5-0.5B model was post-trained using the GRPO (Generalized Reinforcement Learning with Policy Optimization) algorithm on the GSM8K dataset.

GPT-4o was employed as an "LLM-as-a-judge" to classify errors in generated reasoning traces according to the new taxonomy, and Pass@K metrics were extended to evaluate both final answer accuracy and trace coherence.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Arizona State University investigated how Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) influences intermediate reasoning steps in Large Language Models for mathematical tasks. Their study found RLVR improves "trace coherence" by reducing local errors but does not necessarily guarantee "global validity" of the entire reasoning process, highlighting a distinction in its impact on LLM reasoning.

title: Local Coherence or Global Validity? Investigating RLVR Traces in Math Domains

abstract: Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting has become central to mathematical reasoning in large language models, yet models remain brittle to early errors: a single arithmetic slip or unjustified inference typically propagates uncorrected to an incorrect final answer. We investigate whether training on intentionally flawed reasoning traces can teach models to detect and recover from such errors without degrading standard problem-solving ability. Using competition-level problems from MATH-lighteval, we generate CoT prefixes containing exactly one controlled error, either a calculation error (sign flips, dropped terms) or a reasoning error (misapplied rules, unjustified logical steps), and fine-tune Qwen3-4B with GRPO using a binary final-answer reward. Our Mixed-CoT-RL model matches standard RL on clean problems (41% vs 41%) while substantially outperforming it on problems prefilled with flawed reasoning (24% vs 19%). Notably, clean-only RL fine-tuning degrades robustness below the untuned baseline 19% vs. 20%), indicating that conventional training increases susceptibility to misleading prefills. Among error types, training on reasoning errors yields greater robustness gains than calculation errors alone, with mixed training performing best. These findings demonstrate that exposure to flawed traces during training can improve error-recovery behavior without sacrificing accuracy, suggesting a path toward more robust mathematical reasoning in LLMs.

keyInsights: Explicitly training LLMs on intentionally flawed reasoning traces significantly enhances their ability to detect and recover from errors without degrading their standard problem-solving accuracy on clean problems.
Standard reinforcement learning fine-tuning, when applied only to clean data, can inadvertently decrease an LLM's robustness to perturbed inputs, making it more susceptible to misleading prefixes.

Training on reasoning errors appears more beneficial for general robustness against diverse perturbations than focusing solely on calculation errors, potentially fostering more critical evaluation skills.
originalProblem: Large Language Models exhibit brittleness to early errors in multi-step reasoning, especially in mathematics, where a single mistake can propagate and invalidate an entire solution.

Current LLM training and fine-tuning paradigms, such as RLHF and DPO, primarily optimize for a correct final answer, often leaving intermediate reasoning steps unsupervised and vulnerable to uncorrected errors.

Existing advanced mathematical datasets primarily evaluate problem-solving ability on clean inputs, neglecting the model's capacity to detect and recover from misleading or subtly incorrect reasoning prefixes.
results: The Mixed-CoT-RL model achieved 24% accuracy on perturbed math problems, outperforming the Ablation-RL (standard RL) model's 19% and the untuned baseline's 20%.
Mixed-CoT-RL maintained 41% accuracy on standard clean math problems, matching the performance of the Ablation-RL model and demonstrating no degradation in general problem-solving ability.
All Flawed-CoT training variants showed improved robustness on perturbed problems, with Reasoning-Errors-RL achieving 23% accuracy and Calculation-Errors-RL achieving 21% on this benchmark.
solution: A reinforcement learning pipeline using GRPO was implemented to fine-tune an LLM, specifically Qwen3-4B, with a binary final-answer reward system.
The training dataset was augmented with 'flawed trajectories' created by injecting a single, controlled error (either calculation or reasoning type) at the first step of a Chain-of-Thought.
Model variants were trained on mixtures of clean and flawed reasoning traces, or exclusively on specific error types, to assess their impact on error detection and recovery capabilities.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at MIT developed a reinforcement learning approach to fine-tune Large Language Models (LLMs) by exposing them to intentionally flawed mathematical reasoning traces. This method improved the LLM's accuracy on perturbed math problems from 19% to 24% compared to standard RL, while maintaining performance on clean problems.

title: Can Large Reasoning Models Improve Accuracy on Mathematical Tasks Using Flawed Thinking?

abstract: Reinforcement learning substantially improves reasoning in large language models, but it also tends to lengthen chain of thought outputs and increase computational cost during both training and inference. Though length control methods have been proposed, it remains unclear what the optimal output length is for balancing efficiency and performance. In this work, we compare several length control methods on two models, Qwen3-1.7B Base and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B. Our results indicate that length penalties may hinder reasoning acquisition, while properly tuned length control can improve efficiency for models with strong prior reasoning. By extending prior work to RL trained policies, we identify two failure modes, 1) long outputs increase dispersion, and 2) short outputs lead to under-thinking.

keyInsights: The relationship between output length and reasoning accuracy is qualitatively different depending on the base model's initial reasoning strength: weaker models (Qwen3) show a monotonic increase, while stronger models (DeepSeek) exhibit a non-monotonic relationship with an optimal intermediate length.

Two distinct failure modes drive performance degradation in the non-monotonic trend: 'dispersion' (increased answer entropy) for overly long outputs and 'under-thinking' (insufficient reasoning steps and low mode accuracy) for overly short outputs.

Length penalties can hinder reasoning acquisition in models with lower initial capabilities but can be beneficial for models with pre-existing reasoning by guiding them towards an optimal intermediate output length.
originalProblem: Reinforcement learning-trained Large Language Models (LLMs) often generate excessively long Chain-of-Thought (CoT) outputs, leading to increased computational costs during both training and inference.

Despite various length control methods, a comprehensive understanding of the trade-off between output length and performance is lacking, including whether an optimal length exists and how it depends on the base LLM's reasoning capabilities.
Existing research has not fully empirically characterized the 'failure modes' associated with suboptimal output lengths in RL-trained policies beyond test-time interventions.
results: Qwen3-1.7B-Base demonstrated a monotonically increasing trend, where longer outputs consistently yielded higher accuracy, implying length penalties invariably degraded performance for this model.
DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B exhibited a non-monotonic relationship, with its reasoning performance peaking at an optimal intermediate output length.

For DeepSeek, performance loss in the long-output regime was primarily attributed to increased dispersion (higher answer entropy and lower mode share), while in the short-output regime, it was due to low mode accuracy and insufficient reasoning.
solution: The authors conducted systematic experiments using two LLMs with differing initial reasoning capabilities (Qwen3-1.7B-Base and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B) and compared several state-of-the-art length control methods on mathematical reasoning benchmarks.

They extended a theoretical framework to analyze sampled responses, decomposing accuracy into 'mode accuracy,' 'answer entropy,' and 'mode share' to diagnose distinct error sources in the output distributions.
Training was performed using a modified DAPO setup, and evaluations were robustly conducted across multiple mathematical reasoning tasks with extensive sampling and context length checks.
paper_summary.summary: This research reveals that the optimal output length for reinforcement learning-trained language models is not universal, but rather depends on the base model's inherent reasoning capabilities. Models with weaker initial reasoning show monotonically increasing accuracy with longer outputs, whereas models with stronger pre-existing reasoning exhibit a non-monotonic relationship, peaking at an intermediate length due to distinct failure modes at extreme lengths.

title: On the Optimal Reasoning Length for RL-Trained Language Models

abstract: Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) show that extending the length of reasoning chains significantly improves performance on complex tasks. While revealing these reasoning traces helps users better follow, verify, and learn from the model's problem-solving process, it also makes them highly vulnerable to unauthorized distillation. To mitigate this risk, proprietary model providers often adopt aggressive protection strategies, such as replacing detailed reasoning with brief summaries, which deprive users of valuable intermediate information. To address this trade-off, we propose PART, an information-preserving antidistillation reformulation of reasoning traces. Motivated by the difference between how humans understand reasoning traces and how LLMs exploit them for supervised fine-tuning, we design a simple but effective two-step reformulation: removing self-talk behaviors and reordering sub-conclusions. A small auxiliary model is trained to perform this reformulation, incurring minimal computational overhead. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PART consistently disrupts distillation across student models of different sizes and types on various reasoning benchmarks. For instance, when training on reformulated traces, even the performance of a large 32B student model decreases from 54.17 to 46.88 on AIME 2024, corresponding to a 13.5% degradation.
title: Information-Preserving Reformulation of Reasoning Traces for Antidistillation

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive mathematical reasoning capabilities, yet their performance remains brittle to minor variations in problem description and prompting strategy. Furthermore, reasoning is vulnerable to sampling-induced errors which autoregressive models must primarily address using self-correction via additionally-generated tokens. To better understand self-correction capabilities of recent models, we conduct experiments measuring models' ability to self-correct synthetic perturbations introduced into their Chain of Thought (CoT) reasoning. We observe robust single-utterance intrinsic self-correction behavior across a range of open-weight models and datasets, ranging from subtle, implicit corrections to explicit acknowledgments and corrections of errors. Our findings suggest that LLMs, including those not finetuned for long CoT, may possess stronger intrinsic self-correction capabilities than commonly shown in the literature. The presence of this ability suggests that recent "reasoning" model work involves amplification of traits already meaningfully present in models.

keyInsights: The ability to detect and recover from internal inconsistencies appears to be a widespread intrinsic trait in large language models, extending beyond models explicitly finetuned for self-correction.

While present, self-correction capabilities are often imperfect, with most models experiencing substantial accuracy drops (average 61.6% absolute drop) when errors are synthetically introduced, though larger models (>30B) show more robustness.

Self-correction can either be tightly coupled with a model's characteristic generation style (where performance degrades with off-policy stubs) or decoupled, allowing for robust re-initiation of reasoning regardless of the stub's origin.
originalProblem: Large Language Models' reasoning capabilities are often brittle and sensitive to minor changes, leading to unreliable outputs in practical applications.
Existing self-correction methods for LLMs often rely on extrinsic feedback, tool augmentation, or multi-turn prompt-based refinement, which introduce latency, complexity, or computational overhead.
Models frequently struggle to identify and rectify their own errors or contradictions within a single, continuous reasoning process.
results: All evaluated models demonstrated some level of single-utterance intrinsic self-correction, indicating a ubiquitous capability across diverse architectures and sizes.

Most models, excluding the exceptionally performing R1 (671B), experienced significant accuracy degradation in perturbed scenarios, with smaller models (below 30B) showing an average absolute success rate drop of 78.1%.
The R1 (671B) model showed exceptional robustness, maintaining high performance with minimal degradation (e.g., 87.0% off-policy on GSM8K) even when presented with perturbed reasoning stubs.
solution: The authors devised a four-phase experimental workflow: stub generation by the evaluated model, perturbation of the stub by a held-out LLM, completion of the perturbed stub by the original evaluated model in a single utterance, and final grading by a held-out LLM.
They evaluated seven diverse large language models, ranging from 7B to 671B parameters, across three mathematical reasoning datasets: GSM8K, GSM-Symbolic, and MATH-500.

The study compared model performance in direct solution generation (baseline), self-correction from on-policy perturbed stubs, and self-correction from off-policy common perturbed stubs to isolate self-correction capabilities.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at UC Santa Cruz and Cohere empirically demonstrate that modern large language models can perform single-utterance intrinsic self-correction, recovering from synthetically introduced reasoning errors within a continuous generation. This capability is observed across various models and datasets, suggesting it is a widespread intrinsic trait, particularly robust in larger general-purpose and specialized reasoning models, such as DeepSeek's R1, which showed minimal degradation on perturbed tasks.

title: Language Models can perform Single-Utterance Self-Correction of Perturbed Reasoning

abstract: Distilling the thinking traces of a Large Language Model (LLM) with reasoning capabilities into a smaller model has been proven effective. Yet, there is a scarcity of work done on how model performances scale with the quantity of distillation data. In this work, we study the scaling trend of distilling competitive coding skills on two small non-reasoning LLMs. We validate the hypothesis that there is a $\textit{valley of code reasoning}$: downstream performance on competitive coding first drops as data quantity increases, then it steadily increases in a sharper-than-log-linear fashion. Having identified the trend, we further fine-tune the models at two different distillation stages on the same data to ground conclusions on their respective learning phases. We learn that across stages in the low and medium-low data regimes, small models benefit significantly from easier coding questions than from harder ones. We also find that, surprisingly, the correctness of outputs in training data makes no difference to distillation outcomes. Our work represents a step forward in understanding the training dynamics of code reasoning distillation outside intuition
title: The Valley of Code Reasoning: Scaling Knowledge Distillation of Large Language Models

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning have achieved strong performance across diverse tasks, including mathematics, coding, and general reasoning. A distinctive ability of these reasoning models is self-reflection: the ability to review and revise previous reasoning steps. While self-reflection enhances reasoning performance, it also increases inference cost. In this work, we study self-reflection through the lens of representation engineering. We segment the model's reasoning into steps, identify the steps corresponding to reflection, and extract a reflection direction in the latent space that governs this behavior. Using this direction, we propose a stepwise steering method that can control reflection frequency. We call our framework ReflCtrl. Our experiments show that (1) in many cases reflections are redundant, especially in stronger models (in our experiments, we can save up to 33.6 percent of reasoning tokens while preserving performance), and (2) the model's reflection behavior is highly correlated with an internal uncertainty signal, implying self-reflection may be controlled by the model's uncertainty.

keyInsights: A specific "reflection direction" exists within LLM latent space, enabling mechanistic control over self-reflection frequency.
Self-reflection in stronger LLMs often exhibits redundancy, allowing for significant inference cost reductions (up to 33.6% token savings) with minimal impact on accuracy.
The model's internal uncertainty is highly correlated with its self-reflection behavior, suggesting reflection is an emergent mechanism for dealing with task difficulty or low confidence.
The "stepwise steering" intervention method is crucial for maintaining model accuracy and robustness when manipulating internal representations.
originalProblem: Advanced LLM reasoning, particularly with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and native reflection, incurs substantial inference costs, consuming 25-30% of reasoning tokens.
The internal mechanisms that trigger and govern LLM self-reflection remain largely unexplored.
Existing methods primarily offer crude, token-level suppression of reflection, lacking fine-grained control or mechanistic understanding.
results: ReflCtrl successfully reduced reasoning tokens by up to 33.6% on MMLU subsets for DeepSeek-R1 Qwen 14B and by 32.4% on GSM8k for QwQ-32B, with marginal accuracy losses (e.g., 0.14% on GSM8k).
A logistic regression classifier, trained on features derived from the reflection direction, achieved an AUROC of 0.850 in predicting answer correctness, outperforming a baseline classifier (0.716).

The proposed stepwise steering method consistently maintained model accuracy at high intervention strengths, significantly outperforming a standard all-token steering approach which led to performance degradation.
solution: Identify and extract a "reflection direction" in the LLM's latent representation space by calculating the mean difference between embeddings of reflection and non-reflection steps.
Implement a novel "stepwise steering" method that applies intervention vectors only at the beginning of new thinking steps, balancing control with performance preservation.
Propose quantifying model uncertainty by training a classifier on features derived from the projection onto the reflection direction to predict answer correctness.
paper_summary.summary: The ReflCtrl framework, developed by researchers at UCSD, utilizes representation engineering to identify and steer self-reflection in large language models by manipulating a "reflection direction" in their latent space. This approach achieved up to a 33.6% reduction in reasoning tokens on benchmark tasks with marginal accuracy loss, revealing a strong correlation between reflection and the model's internal uncertainty signals.

title: ReflCtrl: Controlling LLM Reflection via Representation Engineering

abstract: Eliciting "chain of thought" (CoT) rationales -- sequences of token that convey a "reasoning" process -- has been shown to consistently improve LLM performance on tasks like question answering. More recent efforts have shown that such rationales can also be used for model distillation: Including CoT sequences (elicited from a large "teacher" model) in addition to target labels when fine-tuning a small student model yields (often substantial) improvements. In this work we ask: Why and how does this additional training signal help in model distillation? We perform ablations to interrogate this, and report some potentially surprising results. Specifically: (1) Placing CoT sequences after labels (rather than before) realizes consistently better downstream performance -- this means that no student "reasoning" is necessary at test time to realize gains. (2) When rationales are appended in this way, they need not be coherent reasoning sequences to yield improvements; performance increases are robust to permutations of CoT tokens, for example. In fact, (3) a small number of key tokens are sufficient to achieve improvements equivalent to those observed when full rationales are used in model distillation.

keyInsights: The effectiveness of CoT-augmented distillation does not necessarily stem from the student model learning to explicitly mimic coherent reasoning.
Appending CoT rationales after the target label during distillation is more effective than prepending them, suggesting a different mechanism of knowledge transfer.
The coherence and full length of CoT rationales are largely irrelevant for performance gains when appended post-label; a small set of gradient-attributed tokens suffices.
originalProblem: The empirical success of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) augmented distillation was established, but the underlying mechanisms explaining its effectiveness remained largely unexplored.
There was a prevailing, often implicit, assumption that student models learned to mimic the teacher's explicit step-by-step reasoning process when CoT rationales were included.
It was unclear whether CoT rationales provided genuinely useful informational content or simply served as 'extra compute' during the distillation process.
results: Distillation with CoT rationales appended after labels consistently outperformed prepended CoT across all student models and datasets (e.g., GPT-2 on CommonsenseQA improved from 67.20% to 70.92% when CoT was appended).
Student model performance benefits were sustained even with high levels of CoT masking (>60%) or shuffling when rationales were appended post-label, but coherence was crucial for prepended CoT.
Using only the top 15 tokens identified by integrated gradients, when appended to labels, yielded performance gains comparable to using full-length CoT rationales.
solution: The authors from Northeastern University employed a systematic teacher-student distillation setup, using Mistral-7B-Instruct as the teacher and GPT-2, Phi-1.5, and Gemma-2B as student models.
They conducted extensive experimental ablations on three commonsense reasoning datasets (CommonsenseQA, OpenbookQA, QuaRel) to test the importance of CoT rationale placement, coherence, and content.
Methods included comparing pre-label and post-label CoT, shuffling and masking CoT tokens, and using automated gradient attribution or human annotations to identify key tokens for distillation.
paper_summary.summary: This research investigates the mechanisms behind performance improvements in model distillation when augmented with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) rationales. It reveals that appending CoT rationales after the target label during training yields superior performance compared to prepending them, and these benefits persist even when the rationales are incoherent or heavily masked. The findings suggest that the student model uses CoT as an enriched training signal rather than learning to explicitly mimic step-by-step reasoning, with a small subset of gradient-attributed tokens being sufficient for knowledge transfer.

title: Investigating Mysteries of CoT-Augmented Distillation

abstract: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning has become the standard paradigm for enabling Large Language Models (LLMs) to solve complex problems. However, recent studies reveal a sharp performance drop in reasoning hop generalization scenarios, where the required number of reasoning steps exceeds training distributions while the underlying algorithm remains unchanged. The internal mechanisms driving this failure remain poorly understood. In this work, we conduct a systematic study on tasks from multiple domains, and find that errors concentrate at token positions of a few critical error types, rather than being uniformly distributed. Closer inspection reveals that these token-level erroneous predictions stem from internal competition mechanisms: certain attention heads, termed erroneous processing heads (ep heads), tip the balance by amplifying incorrect reasoning trajectories while suppressing correct ones. Notably, removing individual ep heads during inference can often restore the correct predictions. Motivated by these insights, we propose test-time correction of reasoning, a lightweight intervention method that dynamically identifies and deactivates ep heads in the reasoning process. Extensive experiments across different tasks and LLMs show that it consistently improves reasoning hop generalization, highlighting both its effectiveness and potential.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China
State Key Laboratory of Cognitive Intelligence

title: Scaling Reasoning Hop Exposes Weaknesses: Demystifying and Improving Hop Generalization in Large Language Models

abstract: Reasoning LLMs (RLLMs) generate step-by-step chains of thought (CoTs) before giving an answer, which improves performance on complex tasks and makes reasoning more transparent. But how robust are these reasoning traces to disruptions that occur within them? To address this question, we introduce a controlled evaluation framework that perturbs a model's own CoT at fixed timesteps. We design seven interventions (benign, neutral, and adversarial) and apply them to multiple open-weight RLLMs across Math, Science, and Logic tasks. Our results show that RLLMs are generally robust, reliably recovering from diverse perturbations, with robustness improving with model size and degrading when interventions occur early. However, robustness is not style-invariant: paraphrasing suppresses doubt-like expressions and reduces performance, while other interventions trigger doubt and support recovery. Recovery also carries a cost: neutral and adversarial noise can inflate CoT length by more than 200%, whereas paraphrasing shortens traces but harms accuracy. These findings provide new evidence on how RLLMs maintain reasoning integrity, identify doubt as a central recovery mechanism, and highlight trade-offs between robustness and efficiency that future training methods should address.

keyInsights: RLLMs exhibit impressive inherent robustness and recovery capabilities, largely attributed to the consistent insertion of metacognitive 'doubt expressions' (e.g., 'Wait') that signal self-questioning after disruptions.

Stylistic changes, such as paraphrasing the reasoning trace, are uniquely disruptive; they suppress doubt expressions, significantly degrade performance, and reveal that RLLMs are not style-invariant.

Robustness often incurs a high computational cost, as models frequently extend their CoT length significantly (e.g., over 200% for neutral, over 400% for some adversarial interventions) to recover from errors.
originalProblem: Reasoning Large Language Models (RLLMs) improve performance on complex tasks but are prone to mistakes and hallucinations, often struggling with reliable error identification and self-correction.

Previous robustness research primarily focused on noisy in-context examples or initial misleading Chain-of-Thought (CoT) parts, rather than dynamic, localized perturbations within the model's own unfolding reasoning trace.
A gap existed in understanding the runtime robustness and the computational cost associated with RLLM recovery from internal, dynamic disruptions during active reasoning.
results: RLLMs consistently recovered from most benign, neutral, and adversarial interventions with near-perfect robustness, with larger models generally exhibiting greater resilience.
The 'Paraphrasing reasoning' intervention consistently led to the lowest robustness scores across models, indicating its unique disruptive impact.
Neutral and adversarial interventions led to substantial inflation of CoT length (e.g., some instances exceeding 200-400%), representing a significant computational overhead for recovery.
solution: A controlled evaluation framework was developed to systematically perturb RLLMs' generated Chains-of-Thought (CoTs) at five fixed timesteps (0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9 fractional CoT length).
Seven distinct intervention types (benign, neutral, adversarial) were applied to individual reasoning steps of nine open-weight RLLMs across MATH, SCIENCE, and LOGIC domains.
Robustness was measured by final answer correctness at three strictness levels, and the computational cost of recovery was quantified through CoT length inflation and analysis of 'doubt expressions'.
paper_summary.summary: Research from Helmholtz Munich, LMU, and TUM investigated the robustness of Reasoning LLMs to dynamic, localized interventions within their Chain-of-Thought reasoning processes. It found that while RLLMs exhibit high recovery rates and self-correction via "doubt expressions," stylistic paraphrasing significantly degrades performance and suppresses these metacognitive signals, often at a substantial computational cost for recovery.

title: Are Reasoning LLMs Robust to Interventions on Their Chain-of-Thought?

abstract:
Uncertainty estimation is critical for deploying reasoning language models, yet remains poorly understood under extended chain-of-thought reasoning. We study parallel sampling as a fully black-box approach using verbalized confidence and self-consistency. Across three reasoning models and 17 tasks spanning mathematics, STEM, and humanities, we characterize how these signals scale.
Both self-consistency and verbalized confidence scale in reasoning models, but self-consistency exhibits lower initial discrimination and lags behind verbalized confidence under moderate sampling. Most uncertainty gains, however, arise from signal combination: with just two samples, a hybrid estimator improves AUROC by up to $+12$ on average and already outperforms either signal alone even when scaled to much larger budgets, after which returns diminish. These effects are domain-dependent: in mathematics, the native domain of RLVR-style post-training, reasoning models achieve higher uncertainty quality and exhibit both stronger complementarity and faster scaling than in STEM or humanities.

keyInsights: Both Verbalized Confidence (VC) and Self-Consistency (SC) scale with parallel sampling in Reasoning Language Models (RLMs), but SC consistently trails VC in discriminative power.
The complementarity between VC and SC, when combined into an SCVC hybrid estimator, yields substantially greater gains in uncertainty estimation than scaling either signal alone.
Uncertainty signal scaling and complementarity exhibit strong domain dependence, with mathematical tasks showing the fastest improvements and most effective signal combination.
originalProblem: Uncertainty estimation is critical for reliable RLM deployment, but multi-sample behavior and the interaction between different uncertainty signals were largely uncharacterized.
Insights from standard Large Language Models (LLMs) do not directly transfer to Reasoning Language Models (RLMs) due to their extensive internal deliberation and longer reasoning traces.
The high computational cost of generating multiple RLM chain-of-thought traces necessitates efficient uncertainty estimation strategies.
results: Combining SC and VC with only two samples ($K=2$) resulted in an average AUROC of 84.2 in mathematics, a +12.9 AUROC improvement over single-sample VC.
The SCVC at $K=2$ consistently outperformed the peak performance of either VC or SC when scaled to eight samples ($K=8$) across all tested domains.
In STEM and humanities, $K=2$ SCVC improved over single-sample VC by +6.4 AUROC and surpassed the strongest single-signal estimates at $K=8$ by +1.8 to +2.3 AUROC.
solution: The study systematically evaluated three black-box uncertainty signals: Verbalized Confidence (VC), Self-Consistency (SC), and their linear combination (SCVC), across increasing sampling budgets and various task domains.

Three open-source reasoning models were tested on 17 tasks spanning mathematics, STEM (non-math), and humanities, using Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve (AUROC) as the primary evaluation metric.

A bootstrap evaluation protocol was implemented to account for stochastic decoding variance and maximize data utilization, alongside analyses of weighting parameter sensitivity and signal correlation.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the University of Tartu empirically characterized black-box uncertainty estimation in Reasoning Language Models, demonstrating that combining verbalized confidence and self-consistency with just two samples provides superior and more cost-effective uncertainty discrimination than scaling either signal individually. The study revealed strong domain dependence, with mathematical tasks showing the most pronounced benefits from these combined signals.

title: How Uncertainty Estimation Scales with Sampling in Reasoning Models

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) trained via Reinforcement Learning (RL) have recently achieved impressive results on reasoning benchmarks. Yet, growing evidence shows that these models often generate longer but ineffective chains of thought (CoTs), calling into question whether benchmark gains reflect real reasoning improvements. We present new evidence of overthinking, where models disregard correct solutions even when explicitly provided, instead continuing to generate unnecessary reasoning steps that often lead to incorrect conclusions. Experiments on three state-of-the-art models using the AIME2024 math benchmark reveal critical limitations in these models ability to integrate corrective information, posing new challenges for achieving robust and interpretable reasoning.

keyInsights: LLM "reasoning" appears unreliable and resistant to external correction, questioning the authenticity of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) as a genuine internal thought process.
Current proxy metrics like completion length are insufficient and often misleading indicators of reasoning quality, as models exhibit both "underthinking" and "overthinking".
The observed behavior likely stems from overfitting to specific patterns and spurious correlations, potentially due to Reinforcement Learning (RL) training dynamics and poor credit assignment.
originalProblem: Growing skepticism exists about the authenticity and robustness of Large Language Models' (LLMs) perceived reasoning capabilities, particularly those employing Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting.
LLMs often produce ineffective CoTs, exhibit 'overthinking' by generating excessive, unproductive tokens, and anecdotally disregard correct solutions even when explicitly provided.
It is unclear whether impressive benchmark gains genuinely reflect improved reasoning or are artifacts of training dynamics, rather than coherent information processing.
results: Across all tested models (LLaMA 70B, Qwen7B, DeepScalR1.5B), models consistently disregarded explicitly provided correct solutions, instead generating widespread "overthinking" with excessive, redundant reasoning steps.
Acceptance of ground truth was extremely rare, observed only twice across all experiments, and often appeared to be due to "resignation or time pressure" rather than genuine understanding.
Models frequently exhibited "divergent behavior," committing to incorrect answers even after being presented with the correct solution, with the number of overthinking tokens growing considerably.
solution: The researchers employed a novel counterfactual experimental setup to probe reasoning dynamics in state-of-the-art LLMs, focusing on their ability to integrate external ground truth.
For each problem in the AIME2024 math benchmark, a baseline Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning trajectory was first generated by the models.

The ground truth solution (reasoning steps and final answer) was then programmatically concatenated to the model's generated thinking trace at various intermediate steps, and the model's subsequent tokens were analyzed for overthinking and acceptance.
paper_summary.summary: This study investigates the reliability of Large Language Model reasoning trajectories. It demonstrates that advanced LLMs frequently disregard explicitly provided correct solutions, leading to excessive "overthinking" and revealing limitations in their ability to integrate corrective information, particularly in complex mathematical problems.

title: Large Reasoning Models are not thinking straight: on the unreliability of thinking trajectories

abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly deployed in high-stakes settings where reliable uncertainty quantification (UQ) is as important as predictive accuracy. Extended reasoning via chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting or reasoning-trained models has become ubiquitous in modern VLM pipelines, yet its effect on UQ reliability remains poorly understood. We show that reasoning consistently degrades the quality of most uncertainty estimates, even when it improves task accuracy. We identify implicit answer conditioning as the primary mechanism: as reasoning traces converge on a conclusion before the final answer is generated, token probabilities increasingly reflect consistency with the model's own reasoning trace rather than uncertainty about correctness. In effect, the model becomes overconfident in its answer. In contrast, agreement-based consistency remains robust and often improves under reasoning, making it a practical choice for uncertainty estimation in reasoning-enabled VLMs.

keyInsights: Chain-of-Thought reasoning consistently degrades the quality of uncertainty estimates reliant on answer-token likelihoods in VLMs, even when overall task accuracy improves.

The primary driver for this degradation is "implicit answer conditioning," where the reasoning trace's internal semantic convergence inflates the model's confidence in its final answer, irrespective of its correctness or visual ambiguity.
Agreement-based consistency methods for uncertainty estimation remain robust or even improve under reasoning, offering a viable solution for UQ in reasoning-enabled VLMs.
originalProblem: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) require robust Uncertainty Quantification (UQ) for reliable deployment in high-stakes applications, enabling them to abstain when uncertain.

While Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning generally improves VLM task accuracy, its specific impact on the reliability of uncertainty estimates, especially in multimodal contexts, was largely unexplored.
Existing UQ benchmarks and methods often do not systematically investigate how reasoning modifies uncertainty ranking reliability in multimodal selective generation scenarios.
results: Answer-Token Likelihood (ATL) based uncertainty estimates (e.g., MSP, PPL, MTE) consistently showed substantial deterioration in ranking quality (PRR and Spearman correlation) with CoT prompting.

Agreement-based "Consistency" estimates remained reliable and often demonstrated improved ranking quality under CoT reasoning and in intrinsically reasoning-trained models, outperforming other UQ methods.

"Implicit answer conditioning" was strongly supported by evidence: ATL-based confidence correlated positively with final answer frequency in reasoning traces, and masking explicit answer mentions significantly restored ATL-based UQ quality.
solution: Evaluated a comprehensive range of uncertainty estimation strategies, including Answer-Token Likelihood, Self-Probing, and Agreement-Based methods, within a selective generation framework.
Assessed the quality of uncertainty estimates using metrics like Prediction Rejection Ratio (PRR) and Spearman correlation, which isolate ranking reliability from predictive accuracy.
Investigated mechanisms by analyzing reasoning trace length, final answer frequency within reasoning traces, and a controlled masking intervention to understand "implicit answer conditioning."
paper_summary.summary: Chain-of-Thought reasoning in vision-language models enhances performance but systematically diminishes the reliability of likelihood-based uncertainty estimates. Researchers at KTH Royal Institute of Technology found this degradation stems from "implicit answer conditioning," where the reasoning trace inflates confidence, yet agreement-based uncertainty methods remain robust or improve.

title: The Cost of Reasoning: Chain-of-Thought Induces Overconfidence in Vision-Language Models

abstract: Reasoning LLMs are trained to verbalize their reasoning process, yielding strong gains on complex tasks. This transparency also opens a promising direction: multiple reasoners can directly collaborate on each other's thinking within a shared trajectory, yielding better inference efficiency and exploration. A key prerequisite, however, is the ability to assess the usefulness and build on another model's partial thinking -- we call this off-trajectory reasoning. Our paper investigates a critical question: can standard solo-reasoning training pipelines deliver desired off-trajectory behaviors? We propose twin tests that capture the two extremes of the off-trajectory spectrum, namely Recoverability, which tests whether LLMs can backtrack from "distractions" induced by misleading reasoning traces, and Guidability, which tests their ability to build upon correct reasoning from stronger collaborators. Our study evaluates 15 open-weight LLMs (1.5B-32B) and reveals a counterintuitive finding -- "stronger" LLMs on benchmarks are often more fragile under distraction. Moreover, all models tested fail to effectively leverage guiding steps from collaborators on problems beyond their inherent capabilities with solve rates remaining under 9.2%. Finally, we conduct control studies to isolate the effects of three factors in post-training on these behaviors: the choice of distillation teacher, the use of RL, and data selection strategy. Our results provide actionable insights for training natively strong reasoning collaborators; e.g., we find that suboptimal recoverability behaviors of teacher models are transferred to distilled students even if the distillation trajectories are correct. Taken together, this work lays the groundwork for evaluating multi-model collaborations in shared reasoning trajectories and highlights the limitations of off-the-shelf reasoning LLMs.
title: Off-Trajectory Reasoning: Can LLMs Collaborate on Reasoning Trajectory?

abstract: Recent studies suggest that self-reflective prompting can significantly enhance the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, the use of external feedback as a stop criterion raises doubts about the true extent of LLMs' ability to emulate human-like self-reflection. In this paper, we set out to clarify these capabilities under a more stringent evaluation setting in which we disallow any kind of external feedback. Our findings under this setting show a split: while self-reflection enhances performance in TruthfulQA, it adversely affects results in HotpotQA. We conduct follow-up analyses to clarify the contributing factors in these patterns, and find that the influence of self-reflection is impacted both by reliability of accuracy in models' initial responses, and by overall question difficulty: specifically, self-reflection shows the most benefit when models are less likely to be correct initially, and when overall question difficulty is higher. We also find that self-reflection reduces tendency toward majority voting. Based on our findings, we propose guidelines for decisions on when to implement self-reflection. We release the codebase for reproducing our experiments at this https URL.
title: When Hindsight is Not 20/20: Testing Limits on Reflective Thinking in Large Language Models

abstract: While LLMs have seen substantial improvement in reasoning capabilities, they also sometimes overthink, generating unnecessary reasoning steps, particularly under uncertainty, given ill-posed or ambiguous queries. We introduce statistically principled early stopping methods that monitor uncertainty signals during generation to mitigate this issue. Our first approach is parametric: it models inter-arrival times of uncertainty keywords as a renewal process and applies sequential testing for stopping. Our second approach is nonparametric and provides finite-sample guarantees on the probability of halting too early on well-posed queries. We conduct empirical evaluations on reasoning tasks across several domains and models. Our results indicate that uncertainty-aware early stopping can improve both efficiency and reliability in LLM reasoning, and we observe especially significant gains for math reasoning.
title: Statistical Early Stopping for Reasoning Models

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities in reasoning, positioning them as promising tools for supporting human problem-solving. However, what happens when their performance is affected by misinformation, i.e., incorrect inputs introduced by users due to oversights or gaps in knowledge? Such misinformation is prevalent in real-world interactions with LLMs, yet how it propagates within LLMs' reasoning process remains underexplored. Focusing on mathematical reasoning, we present a comprehensive analysis of how misinformation affects intermediate reasoning steps and final answers. We also examine how effectively LLMs can correct misinformation when explicitly instructed to do so. Even with explicit instructions, LLMs succeed less than half the time in rectifying misinformation, despite possessing correct internal knowledge, leading to significant accuracy drops (10.02% - 72.20%), and the degradation holds with thinking models (4.30% - 19.97%). Further analysis shows that applying factual corrections early in the reasoning process most effectively reduces misinformation propagation, and fine-tuning on synthesized data with early-stage corrections significantly improves reasoning factuality. Our work offers a practical approach to mitigating misinformation propagation.
title: Unraveling Misinformation Propagation in LLM Reasoning

abstract: A prevailing narrative in LLM post-training holds that supervised finetuning (SFT) memorizes while reinforcement learning (RL) generalizes. We revisit this claim for reasoning SFT with long chain-of-thought (CoT) supervision and find that cross-domain generalization is not absent but conditional, jointly shaped by optimization dynamics, training data, and base-model capability. Some reported failures are under-optimization artifacts: cross-domain performance first degrades before recovering and improving with extended training (a dip-and-recovery pattern), so shorttraining checkpoints can underestimate generalization.
